article_title: Johnson & Johnson's OTTAVA Robotic Surgical System Meets Pivotal Study Endpoints in Gastric Bypass Trial

content_preview.title: Johnson & Johnson's OTTAVA Robotic Surgical System Meets Pivotal Study Endpoints in Gastric Bypass Trial
content_preview.summary: J&J's table-integrated OTTAVA robot completed all 30 gastric bypass procedures without conversion and met safety endpoints, advancing its FDA De Novo application.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

Johnson & Johnson announced on May 5, 2026, that its investigational OTTAVA™ Robotic Surgical System met the primary safety and performance endpoints of the FORTE clinical study, a prospective, multicenter evaluation of the system in Roux-en-Y gastric bypass procedures, according to [a press release published on BioSpace](https://www.biospace.com/press-releases/johnson-johnson-announces-pivotal-clinical-study-results-for-a-new-soft-tissue-surgical-robotic-system). Results were prese...

editor_notes.content_quality: Well-structured News article covering the FORTE clinical study results for J&J's OTTAVA robotic surgical system. Appropriate use of the What We Know / What We Don't Know / Analysis format. The analysis section contextualizes the OTTAVA within the competitive surgical robotics landscape and correctly references prior coverage of CMR Surgical's Versius Plus launch. Word count (846) is within the News range (400-1200).
editor_notes.source_verification: Both sources are BioSpace press releases fetched with HTTP 200 status. Source-0 (source-0.html.gz): Confirms all claims from the FORTE study section — 30-patient cohort, six U.S. sites, all procedures completed robotically without conversion, average 30-lb weight loss at 30 days, OR sizes ranging from 243 to 694 sq ft, five of six sites with no prior robotic surgery, NCT06709261 trial ID, and all three named executive quotes (Wilson, Abouhalka, Schulam). Quotes are verified verbatim. Source-1 (source-1.html.gz): Confirms FDA De Novo submission on January 8, 2026, the targeted upper-abdominal procedure set (gastric bypass, gastric sleeve, small bowel resection, hiatal hernia repair), the separately approved IDE for inguinal hernia, Polyphonic digital ecosystem integration, and Ethicon instrumentation. All claims in the article trace to the cited sources. Internal link to CMR Surgical Versius Plus article verified at src/content/articles/2026-04/23-cmr-surgical-kicks-off-us-launch-of-versius-plus-opening-a-second-front-against-intuitive-surgicals-da-vinci.md.
editor_notes.factual_accuracy: All specifics verified against sources: trial enrollment (30 patients), follow-up period (30 days), number of sites (six U.S. hospitals), OR size range (243–694 sq ft), weight-loss outcome (average 30 lbs), De Novo submission date (January 8, 2026), targeted procedure set, Ethicon instrumentation, Polyphonic ecosystem. No hallucinations or fabricated specifics detected.
editor_notes.tone_assessment: Neutral and professional throughout. No sensationalism or editorializing. The What We Don't Know section appropriately flags study limitations (30-patient single-arm design, short follow-up, narrow indication vs. broad De Novo scope). No AI self-reference.
editor_notes.originality: No prior OTTAVA coverage exists in the archive. The article is a new topic. The internal reference to CMR Surgical's Versius Plus is appropriate context, not duplication.
editor_notes.overall_assessment: Clean, factually accurate submission. All sources verified, all quotes confirmed verbatim, no hallucinated specifics. Approving for publication.
